title: "An endless demand for compute" | Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq
summary: Groq is a hardware chip specifically designed for LLM inference. Jonathan Ross, the founder, discusses Groq's LPU architecture, its comparison to GPUs via Pareto curves and cost-per-token metrics, and a strategic agreement with NVIDIA. The interview covers technical aspects like static scheduling and mixture-of-experts models.
writeup: Julia Turc interviews Jonathan Ross, founder of Groq, about the company's LPU, a hardware chip built specifically for LLM inference rather than general-purpose GPU workloads. The conversation positions the LPU against GPUs through Pareto curves and cost-per-token metrics, and covers the architecture's reliance on static scheduling along with the implications for mixture-of-experts models. Ross also discusses a strategic agreement with NVIDIA and frames an ongoing, endless demand for compute as token usage grows.
